title: 10 best herbal teas for jaw lock
intro_desc: Herbal teas for jaw lock are natural remedies made from a variety of herbs that help alleviate tension and pain in the jaw. Jaw lock, also known as temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder, can cause discomfort and limit jaw movement. Herbal teas like chamomile, valerian root, and skullcap contain anti-inflammatory and muscle-relaxing properties that can help reduce tension in the jaw muscles, providing relief from pain and discomfort. By drinking these herbal teas regularly, individuals can experience improved jaw mobility, reduced headaches, and a better quality of life. For example, a person who suffered from chronic jaw pain and headaches due to jaw lock found significant relief after drinking chamomile tea daily for two weeks, allowing them to enjoy their favorite foods again and improve their overall well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: German chamomile teas helps with jaw lock because of its calming properties that soothe muscle tension in the jaw, neck, and facial areas. The tea's natural anti-inflammatory compounds also reduce swelling and discomfort associated with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders, which often cause jaw locking. Additionally, chamomile's sedative effects promote relaxation, allowing for a more peaceful night's sleep, which can help alleviate morning jaw pain and stiffness. By addressing these underlying causes, German chamomile teas provide relief from jaw lock and related discomforts.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: German Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, can reduce swelling and ease pain in the jaw joint, helping to relieve tension and lockjaw.

Calming effects: The calming flavonoids and terpenoids in German Chamomile tea can help to relax the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw, reducing stress and anxiety that may contribute to jaw locking.
Antispasmodic properties: German Chamomile's antispasmodic compounds can help to relieve muscle spasms and cramps in the jaw and surrounding areas, providing relief from persistent jaw locking.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in sesquiterpenes and flavonoids, which help to reduce inflammation and ease muscle tension in the jaw.
Flowers: Contain apigenin, a flavonoid that has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to alleviate pain and discomfort associated with jaw locking.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers into a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Heat 1 cup of filtered water to a simmer in a kettle or saucepan.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and let steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to catch solids.
Discard solids and serve the tea warm, or chill in the refrigerator for later use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: White willow teas helps with jaw lock because it contains salicin, a natural compound that works similarly to aspirin. When consumed as a tea, salicin is absorbed into the bloodstream and travels to the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw, where it helps to reduce inflammation and relax tense muscles. This can provide relief from the pain and stiffness associated with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders, which often cause jaw lock or clicking joints. Regular consumption of white willow tea may help to alleviate discomfort and promote a smoother jaw function.
remedy_properties: Salicin: Salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, helps to reduce pain and inflammation in the temporomandibular joint (TMJ), which can contribute to jaw lock.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: White willow tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and phenolic acids that help to reduce swelling and inflammation in the TMJ, easing tension and discomfort associated with jaw lock.

Antioxidant properties: The antioxidants present in white willow tea may help to protect the TMJ from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to chronic pain and stiffness in the jaw.
remedy_parts: Roots: Contain salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, which helps to relieve pain and reduce inflammation associated with jaw lock.
Bark: Rich in salicylic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to soothe and calm the discomfort of jaw lock.
remedy_recipe: Gather high-quality dried white willow bark and measure out two teaspoons for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Bring the measured water to a boil in a medium-sized pot before reducing heat to a simmer.
Add the two teaspoons of dried white willow bark to the simmering water and let steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large cup or teapot, discarding the solids.
Let the tea cool to a comfortable drinking temperature before serving hot or chilled, depending on personal preference.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Meadowsweet teas helps with jaw lock because it contains salicin, a compound similar to aspirin that naturally reduces inflammation and relieves pain. When consumed as a tea, meadowsweet's anti-inflammatory properties can help soothe the temporomandibular joint (TMJ), alleviating tension and discomfort associated with jaw locking or clicking. Regular consumption of meadowsweet teas may also promote relaxation and reduce stress, which are common contributors to jaw lock issues.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Meadowsweet tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce swelling and discomfort associated with TMJ (temporomandibular joint) disorders, including jaw locks and tension headaches.
Pain-relieving: The salicylic acid present in meadowsweet tea has analgesic and antipyretic effects, providing relief from pain and discomfort caused by jaw locking, inflammation, and dental issues.
Antioxidant: Meadowsweet tea's antioxidant properties help protect the body from oxidative stress and damage, which can contribute to chronic pain and inflammation in the jaw and surrounding areas.
remedy_parts: Roots: Meadowsweet roots are rich in salicylic acid, a natural pain reliever that helps alleviate jaw tension and lock symptoms.
Bark: The bark of the meadowsweet plant contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that help reduce inflammation and ease joint pain associated with jaw locking.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried meadowsweet flowers and leaves in mid-summer for optimal potency.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried meadowsweet herbs with 8 ounces of boiling water in a tea infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, releasing the subtle floral and sweet flavors.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a teapot or cup, discarding solids.
Serve the tea hot, adding honey or lemon to taste, as desired, within 15-20 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Common lime teas helps with jaw lock because of its unique combination of calming and soothing properties. The herbal blend contains natural ingredients that work together to relax the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw, reducing tension and releasing pressure on the temporomandibular joint (TMJ). As a result, common lime teas can help alleviate symptoms such as clicking, popping, or stiffness associated with jaw lock, promoting a more comfortable and relaxed oral opening.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Antioxidants in common lime tea, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, help neutralize free radicals that can contribute to muscle tension and inflammation in the jaw, reducing the frequency and severity of jaw locks.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in common lime tea, including limonene and α-pinene, may help reduce swelling and pain associated with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders that can cause jaw locking.

Muscle relaxant properties: The natural muscle relaxants found in common lime tea, such as citral and geraniol, may help calm tense muscles in the face and neck, including those involved in jaw movement, reducing spasms and lockjaw episodes.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in alkaloids and other compounds that help relax muscles and reduce spasms, making them effective in relieving jaw lock.
Bark: The bark contains flavonoids and tannins that can help reduce inflammation and ease tension in the jaw muscles.
remedy_recipe: Harvest tilia europaea leaves from healthy trees in spring or summer for optimal flavor.
Rinse the fresh leaves with clean water to remove dirt and debris, then pat dry.
Combine one tablespoon of dried tilia europaea leaves with 8 ounces of boiling water and let steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large mug or teapot, discarding solids.
Add honey to taste, as desired, and serve hot or chilled within an hour of preparation time.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with jaw lock because it contains rosmarinic acid, a natural compound that has a calming effect on the muscles of the face and neck. By soothing these areas, lemon balm tea can help to relax the temporomandibular joint (TMJ), which is responsible for controlling jaw movement. This relaxation can alleviate symptoms of jaw lock, such as pain, stiffness, and limited mobility, allowing for more comfortable and relaxed jaw function.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Lemon balm tea's antispasmodic properties help to relax and calm down muscle spasms in the face and jaw, reducing tension and pain associated with TMJ disorders.
Anxiolytic properties: Lemon balm tea's anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) properties can help to alleviate stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for jaw locking and bruxism (teeth grinding).

Anti-inflammatory properties: Lemon balm tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help to reduce swelling and pain in the jaw joint and surrounding tissues, providing relief from discomfort and inflammation associated with temporomandibular disorders.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the lemon balm plant have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing and relieving jaw pain associated with tension and lockjaw.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes (underground stems) of lemon balm contain compounds that can help to relax tense muscles and reduce spasms in the jaw, providing relief from lockjaw.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves or purchase dried ones from a reputable source.
Measure one teaspoon of dried lemon balm per eight ounces of boiling water.
Combine the measured lemon balm with boiling water in a teapot or infuser and steep for five minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or fine-mesh sieve to remove loose leaves.
Add honey to taste if desired and serve warm or chilled.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Catnip teas helps with jaw lock because of its unique ability to relax the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw. The calming properties of catnip, a natural herb known for its soothing effects, work to ease tension and stiffness in the temporomandibular joint (TMJ), often causing jaw pain and limited movement. By sipping on a warm cup of herbal catnip tea, individuals can experience relief from jaw lock by reducing muscle spasms and promoting a sense of calm.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Herbal catnip tea's anti-inflammatory property helps to reduce swelling and pain associated with jaw lock, making it easier to relax the muscles and restore normal movement.
Muscle relaxant: The muscle-relaxing compounds in catnip tea help to calm down tense muscles in the face and jaw, reducing stiffness and spasms that can contribute to jaw lock.
Anxiolytic: The anxiolytic properties of catnip tea help to reduce stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for jaw lock (also known as temporomandibular joint disorder or TMJ).
remedy_parts: Roots: Used for its calming effects, roots help soothe and relax the muscles in the jaw, providing relief from tension and pain.
Seeds: Containing analgesic properties, seeds are added to teas to reduce inflammation and alleviate discomfort associated with jaw lock.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried catnip leaves measuring one tablespoon per eight ounces water.
Rinse harvested catnip leaves with clean filtered water to remove dirt and debris.
Combine one tablespoon of rinsed catnip leaves with eight ounces boiling water for five minutes steeping.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Allow the tea to cool to a comfortable drinking temperature before serving immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valerian teas helps with jaw lock because its natural calming properties promote relaxation of the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw. The tea's sedative effects help to reduce tension and stress that can cause clenching or locking of the jaw, allowing for a more gentle and relaxed bite. Additionally, valerian's anti-inflammatory properties may also help to soothe any discomfort or pain associated with a locked jaw.
remedy_properties: Sedative and hypnotic properties: Valerian root has been shown to promote relaxation, reduce stress and anxiety, and improve sleep quality, which can help alleviate tension-related jaw lock symptoms.

Muscle relaxant properties: Valerian's active compounds, such as isovaleric acid and valeric acid, have been found to relax muscle spasms and reduce inflammation, making it effective in relieving jaw tension and lockjaw.

Pain-relieving properties: Valerian tea has anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which can help alleviate pain and discomfort associated with jaw lock, such as temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are often used because they contain the highest concentration of essential oils, which provide a soothing and calming effect on the jaw.
Rhyzomes: Valerian rhyzomes have been traditionally used in herbal medicine to ease tension and spasms, making them an effective ingredient for jaw lock teas.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried valeriana officinalis roots and stems from a trusted supplier.
Cut the root material into smaller pieces and rinse with filtered water to remove dirt.
Combine one teaspoon of dried valerian root or two teaspoons of fresh valerian root with 8 ounces boiling water in a teapot.
Steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain and discard the solids using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving and enjoy as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Humulus lupulus teas helps with jaw lock because of its unique properties that relax and soothe the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds reduce tension and stiffness, allowing the jaw to freely move without locking up. Additionally, the calming effects of humulus lupulus tea help alleviate stress and anxiety, common triggers of bruxism and jaw clenching. As a result, this herbal tea provides natural relief from jaw lock and promotes overall oral health and comfort.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: Hops tea's antispasmodic compounds, such as flavonoids and saponins, can relax muscle spasms in the jaw, reducing tension and pain associated with TMJ disorders.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory agents present in hops tea may help reduce swelling and inflammation in the joint, which can contribute to jaw lock symptoms like limited mobility and discomfort.

Anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) properties: Hops tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help alleviate anxiety-related tension that can exacerbate jaw lock issues, such as bruxism or teeth grinding.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in sesquiterpenes, which help reduce muscle spasms and tension in the jaw area.
Bark: Containing compounds like salicin, which acts as a natural anti-inflammatory agent to alleviate jaw pain and stiffness.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2-3 teaspoons of dried humulus lupulus leaves per cup of boiling water.
Bring filtered water to a boil in a pot and pour it over the herb mixture.
Steep the tea for 10-15 minutes depending on desired strength and flavor profile.
Strain the tea using a tea strainer or piece of cheesecloth into a heat-resistant cup.
Discard solids and enjoy warm or let cool to serve chilled.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop teas helps with jaw lock because it contains a unique blend of natural compounds that work together to relax the muscles in the face, neck, and jaw. The tea's soothing properties help to calm tension and reduce inflammation, allowing for improved blood flow and reduced stiffness. This can provide quick relief from jaw locking or grinding, helping to alleviate symptoms such as facial pain and discomfort.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic properties: The antispasmodic compounds in Maypop tea, such as scopoletin and scopoline, help to relax and calm muscle spasms in the jaw, reducing tension and pain associated with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Maypop tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and glycosides that help reduce swelling and inflammation in the jaw, which can contribute to chronic pain and stiffness associated with TMJ disorders.

Muscle relaxant properties: The alkaloids present in Maypop tea, such as pasiternoid and pasiquin, have a sedative effect on the nervous system, helping to calm muscle tension and spasms in the jaw, which can contribute to locked or clenched jaws.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the Maypop plant are rich in calcium and magnesium, which help relax muscles and reduce tension in the jaw.
Bark: The bark of the Maypop plant contains astringent compounds that help shrink swollen tissues and reduce inflammation in the jaw area.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried or fresh maypop flowers and leaves.
Heat one cup of water in a medium saucepan to 212 degrees fahrenheit for five minutes.
Add the maypop herbs to the boiling water and steep for seven to ten minutes.
Strain the tea into a teapot or individual cups using a fine-mesh strainer.
Serve the tea hot or let it cool to enjoy as an herbal infusion.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Skullcap teas helps with jaw lock because it contains flavonoids, which have a calming effect on the muscles. When we experience stress or anxiety, our jaw muscles tighten, leading to jaw lock. The flavonoids in skullcap tea help to relax these muscles, reducing tension and promoting a sense of calm. This can be especially helpful for people who grind their teeth at night, as it allows them to release this tension and sleep more comfortably.
remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Skullcap tea contains flavonoids, which are known for their anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving effects, helping to reduce inflammation and discomfort in the jaw.

Iridoid glycosides: Iridoid glycosides in skullcap tea have been shown to have a sedative effect on muscle tissue, helping to relax tense muscles in the jaw and relieve tension headaches associated with jaw lock.

Scutellarein: Scutellarein is a flavonoid found in skullcap tea that has been reported to have a relaxing effect on smooth muscle, including the masseter muscle in the jaw, which can help to reduce muscle spasms and pain related to jaw lock.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, roots are valued for their anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and swelling associated with jaw lock.
Rhyzomes: Containing sesquiterpenes, rhyzomes have a calming effect on the nervous system, easing tension and spasms that contribute to jaw lock.
remedy_recipe: Gather high-quality dried scutellaria lateriflora herbs and measure out one teaspoon per eight ounces water.
Heat fresh filtered water to boiling point in a teapot or kettle and pour over the measured herb mixture.
Steep the tea for seven to ten minutes, allowing the herbal properties to infuse into the hot water.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids and discard the herbs.
Cool the tea to drinking temperature and serve hot or chilled, with or without honey to taste.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat jaw lock with herbal teas is by using a combination of calming and anti-inflammatory herbs. Chamomile tea, known for its calming properties, can help reduce tension in the jaw muscles. Meanwhile, ginger and turmeric teas have anti-inflammatory effects that can help alleviate pain and inflammation in the affected area. Steep one to two teaspoons of the chosen herb in hot water for 5-10 minutes, then strain and drink. For optimal results, consume 2-3 cups daily. However, if symptoms persist, seek medical attention.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to jaw lock that are treated with herbal teas include temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder and bruxism. TMJ disorder can cause pain and stiffness in the jaw, while bruxism is characterized by teeth grinding or clenching. Herbal teas, such as chamomile and valerian root, can help relax the muscles and reduce inflammation, providing relief for these conditions. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment. Additionally, these teas should not be used as a substitute for any prescribed medication without consulting a healthcare professional.
